Sure, just use `print` or provide a callback that you invoke. Logging doesn't have to be particularly complicated. :-)


> Ah.  This was something I blatantly stole from `flutter_tools`.

Oh dear, we still have code there that does this? I thought we'd excised all of it. Sigh. I'll file a bug or something to deal with that. In the meantime, yeah, let's avoid reproducing this mistake here.


Happy to give a more detailed review, let me know when it's ready. :-)

## [Slider] Custom track, ticker, and overlay shape painters

Create a slider shape for custom track, tick mark, and overlay painting. Originally, this work was part of the larger range slider effort, but can and should work independently of having more than 1 thumb.

Without this change

```
Part             | Custom Painter? | Custom Options?
__________________________________
Track            | ** NO **        | color
Ticks            | ** NO **        | periodicity, color
Thumbs           | YES             | color
Overlay          | ** NO **        | color
Value Indicators | YES             | color, text style

```

With this change

```
Part             | Custom Painter? | Custom Options?
__________________________________
Track            | ** YES **       | color, ** height **
Ticks            | ** YES **       | periodicity, color
Thumbs           | YES             | color
Overlay          | ** YES **       | color
Value Indicators | YES             | color, text style
```

CHANGES:
* SliderTrackShape is added as slider theme param, with a default of RectangularSliderTrackShape, and slider RenderBox paint() is updated to use it.
    * The default track shape uses the slider theme colors to maintain compatibility with the activeTrackColor and inactiveTrackColor params in Slider.
    * The default track shape uses a newly created slider theme param: trackHeight, but is overridden if custom track shape is used.
    * Dependency between overlay, track length, and value mapping is removed from the slider class, and made an artifact of the default track shape.
* SliderComponentShape for ticks is added as a slider theme param, with a default of RoundSliderTickShape, and slider RenderBox paint() is updated to use it.
* All tick marks are now painted, instead of excluding the one with the same center as the thumb. This does not affect the default state, but is important if custom tick marks extend the bounds of a thumb
    * Default tick mark radius is a function of track height.
* SliderComponentShape for overlay is added as a slider theme param, with a default of RoundSliderOverlayShape, and slider RenderBox paint() is updated to use it.
* The new shape API and slider paint() works for a single thumb, but also allows for easy adaptation to multiple thumbs, in preparation for the RangeSlider, without having to change the new public API.

NEXT UP: 
* Update rendering to material spec.
    * Tick marks should be aligned with the center of the value, not adjusted to be contained within the track.
    * Thumb shape default radius should be larger.
* More decoupling and customization.
    * Default overlay shape painting should be a function of thumbShape.
    * Default overlay shape should paint after thumb and be painted as an annulus.
    * Default overlay color should use new color theme.
    * Paint color tweens can be initialized once, rather than on every paint.
    * RangeSlider

closes #22325
Updated, PTAL

## Refactor RenderObject.toStringDeep code to use DiagnosticsNode.

Fixes https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/9568
This is a breaking change in how toStringDeep output is computed for Widget and Element classes. 
All implementations of `debugFillDescription` and `debugDescribeChildren` will  need to be refactored and I am happy to refactor any code people have that needs to be refactored due to this change. Instead of generating raw strings, debug data now consists of DiagnosticsNode objects containing sufficient style information to exactly match existing toStringDeep rendering while saving implementors of debugDescribeChilden and debugFillDescription from having to worry about generating pixel perfect ascii art.

This CL provides lots of examples showing what I believe are best practices of how those methods should be refactored.
I’ll wait until this CL is done to write down the detailed best practices as I expect what I would write would be obsolete after code review iteration polishing the new APIs for specifying structured diagnostics data to better match flutter conventions.

The test expectation changes part of this CL show places I’ve intentionally changed toStringDeep output for the better by fixing unhandled edge cases and inconsistencies between ways the same data was displayed in different toStringDeep methods. There are test cases for all of the complex cases @hixie mentions issue 9568 that I landed in previous test case only CLs.

All core logic for storing structured tree diagnostics data is in tree_diagnostics_mixin.dart. See tree_diagnostics_mixin_test.dart of tests showing how the various property diagnostics descriptions are expected to be used.

All other changes are the refactor of existing debugFillDescription and debugDescribeChildren method.
I would suggest first focusing the review on the how the APIs from tree_diagnostics_mixin.dart are used and how they should be improved to be clearer and more consistent with Flutter style rather than starting with the internals of tree_diagnostics_mixin.dart which is largely just the sausage making to match the existing ascii art exactly.
The APIs to create diagnostic properties (DiagnosticsNode.(doubleProperty|intProperty|stringProperty|objectProperty|enumProperty|...) are ready for review discussion now as those will determine whether this refactor is pleasant for users or places an undue burden of people writing Widget and RenderObject classes. I currently err on the side of being a bit verbose but hopefully unsurprising. I’m happy to refactor the code to use terse top level methods instead of the factory constructors and make more constructors with common default values if desired. 

The general concept is all diagnostic data is represented by DiagnosticsNode objects and each DiagnosticsNode has an optional a list of properties associated with it and a list of children associated with it that are all DiagnosticsNode objects. All DiagnosticsNode object have an associated DiagnosticsTreeStyle (normal|offstage|box|dense|whitespace)  that specifies what general style should be used to display the node in a debugger or as ascii art. DiagnosticNode objects contain name describing their role (e.g. child “child 3” or property  “borderRadius”).

Constructors for DiagnosticsNode are optimized to accept all information that would be useful for both toStringDeep and a future tree debugging view in IntelliJ. As a disclaimer, not all information that could be useful in IntelliJ is currently stored in the DiagnosticsNode objects generated by the constructors in tree_diagnostics_mixin.dart, only generally the data required for toStringDeep. The design is that changes needed to store that information more permanently can be added later with just non-breaking changes to tree_diagnostics_mixin.dart. This avoids adding extra logic now that may not be actually useful for features added to IntelliJ and can trivially be added later.

Search for `enumProperty` in this CL to see all cases where behavior was sometimes intentionally changed so that all enums are treated consistently using dash-separated-enum-values. For example, `lifecycle state: defunct` instead of `_StateLifecycle.defunct`.


The new output is identical for the old output for cases of the form:
```
-    switch (widget.behavior) {
-      case HitTestBehavior.translucent:
-        description.add('behavior: translucent');
-        break;
-      case HitTestBehavior.opaque:
-        description.add('behavior: opaque');
-        break;
-      case HitTestBehavior.deferToChild:
-        description.add('behavior: defer-to-child');
-        break;
+ description.add(new DiagnosticsNode.enumProperty('behavior', widget.behavior, showNull: false));
```
But is different for cases where the toStringDeep methods displayed `HitTestBehavior.deferToChild` directly in the output as was sometimes also the case.

Generally this change should make toStringDeep output more consistent but if there are cases where I changed the output for the worse let me know and I’ll correct them. I believe test coverage is very good for the Render tree code and a bit sparser for the Widget tree code. If desired I can add more property tests for the Widget tree code.

I’ve called out some other odd edge cases in the inline comments.
Specifically:
ticker_provider.dart

Iterable behavior:
Display of zero element iterables seems unhelpful. I’ve set the default be to hide iterables with zero elements to avoid confusion but I can change that.

Before this lands, please coordinate with @apwilson regarding any possible changes needed for Fuchsia.
Please contact @tvolkert for advice on how to roll this into google3.
Please make sure to e-mail flutter-dev@ when this lands to ensure everyone in the community is aware of how to port their code. You may wish to send a heads-up e-mail now to keep everyone in the loop.

The general approach here seems sound. I didn't carefully check the whole patch and am on vacation so probably won't be able to do a detailed check. @cbracken, @tvolkert, or @abarth would all make great substitutes if they're around. Otherwise, hang in there, I'll get to it eventually. :-)
CL is ready for fine grained review.

Closing and reopening as all the comments are causing github to timeout
Also cc @chunhtai

@davidhicks980 A timer doesn't cover all use cases of delaying. The app might want to delay the `showOverlay` call until some event happens, which is not appropriate with a timer.

The developer can throw an error to not call the callback. Or probably better, we can make it a `Future<bool>`. Although it uses an additional boolean information, I think this is the cost we have to pay in order to disallow calling the callback multiple times.

@dkwingsmt I still prefer callbacks. Is there a way we can get an additional opinion on this? This may come down to a matter of preference, but I feel like futures are more constraining in this situation.

It's fine. I have a similar feeling as well. We can stick to callbacks. But still, how do you think of my analysis above about the logic that an opening/closing should invalidate earlier callbacks in order to make fast opening/closing work properly? (which applies to both the future design and the callback design.)

> But still, how do you think of my analysis above about the logic that an opening/closing should invalidate earlier callbacks in order to make fast opening/closing work properly

Clarify this a bit more. What do you mean by fast opening/closing? 

> What happens if a show/hideOverlay from an earlier open/close is called?

The menu would open or close, as expected. For a given menu, showOverlay and hideOverlay are the equivalent of calling _RawMenuAnchorState.open() and _RawMenuAnchorState.close(), which stay the same throughout the lifecycle of a widget.

> What happens if a show/hideOverlay is called for multiple times?

Calling showOverlay on an already-open menu effectively just calls onOpen and setState, which rebuilds the overlayBuilder. 

Calling hideOverlay on an already-closed menu just returns. This means that onClose isn't called. We could change that behavior.

For example, the themed menu is controlled by two buttons, one opens the menu and the other closes the menu.

The `onOpenRequested` is implemented with a 100ms delay followed by a 100ms-long opening animation. The `onCloseRequested` is implemented with a 100ms delay with no closing animation.

Consider the following event sequence, where the user quickly taps open then close.
```
  0ms    The user opens the menu, scheduling a `showOverlay` at 100ms
 60ms    The user closes the menu, scheduling a `hideOverlay` at 160ms
100ms    The themed menu calls `showOverlay` and starts an opening animation that ends at 200ms.
160ms    The themed menu calls `hideOverlay`, which terminates the opening animation and hides the overlay.
```
With the current way, the overlay would appear between 100ms and 160ms. I think this is not desired. Because when the user pressed the closing button, the user intends that the menu should now be closed, and any scheduled opening sequence should be terminated.

I propose that upon `MenuController.close`, all previous `showOverlay`s should be invalidated. Calling them becomes a noop. Same for the other way around.

IMO it's not that hard to understand: a `showOverlay` closure belongs to the `open` that provides it. Subsequent `open/close` calls invalidates earlier `open`, thus invalidating their `showOverlay` closure. Meanwhile, this comes at the cost of boilerplate for almost every widget that uses it.  Handling asynchronous events can be hard and error prone, and it will save implementors a lot of effort by solving them in the base widget, instead of having them figure out `TickerFuture` and/or `Timer` rather than `Future`. 

If we really want to go this direction, it's ok as well, but now that the `show/hideOverlay` between different `onOpen/CloseRequested` calls are indifferent, it makes no sense to only provide them in the callback: the implementers could call them any time, as many times as needed, or even save a callback for calling way later. We should probably make `show/hideOverlay` methods of `MenuController`.

It's not the difficulty of the concept. It's the fact that the function behaves differently depending on when it is called. We could add an assert function to check whether a stale callback is used, but I don't think the functions should be made noop.

`showOverlay` and `hideOverlay` don't affect the position of a menu's animation or delay, so I think expanding the MenuController api would lead to confusion. For example, a menu could stay invisible after calling showOverlay, which is confusing. I also don't think we should try to add additional methods to an API unless it's absolutely necessary for what we are trying to accomplish with this PR.

You're probably right. Keeping `MenuController`'s interface friendly to app developers, instead of also for package developers, is good for clarity. As for `show/hideOverlay`, although they're usable outside of `onXxxRequested` calls, our documentation should have been clear enough that package developers are supposed to use them correctly.
@chunhtai Can you take another look?

@dkwingsmt Sounds good, although we can always expand the MenuController api with future PRs. I'm still curious whether anyone will ask for a way to observe whether a menu anchor is opening/closing... we shall see. 

Anyways, thanks for the feedback! I think we landed on a solid design, so I'm excited to see what the community does with it.
Note: I just added a change to reduce the number of scrolling/resizing subscriptions. No tests broke, and it should be effectively the same code.
@davidhicks980 making the `MenuController` _final_ just broke our code, we were extending the `MenuController` to add some animation functionality. Was this breaking change really intended?

@Albert221 Yes.. although it would have been better if I had included the change in the original RawMenuAnchor PR. The main reason for making MenuController final is that it is now accessible via an inherited widget, MenuController.of(context), so subclasses wouldn't be typed by those using the inherited logic. There're a lot of likes on your comment though, so I'll check with the team to see if I should roll back.
Is the menuControllerOf a big part of the logic? IIRC it's only a convenient getter for developers, right?
Yeah I think removing the `final` class modifier is a good call!

## Improve fidelity of multi-pointer drag gestures.

Improve fidelity of multi-pointer drag gestures.
Hi there! 
My name is winctoryang and fixed the related issue below. Please have a review and have a nice one!
Thank you!

As https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/38926  said: 

**When multi pointers dragging: delta(combined) = sum of delta(n)**
  This is very wrong. When multiple pointers are moving in the same direction, the scrollable will be moved at double or triple the speed.
**When a pointer leaves: all remaining pointers still work.**

The results that I fixed (refer to iOS behavior):

**When dragging**: 
  delta(combined) = max(i of n that are positive) delta(i) - max(i of n that are negative) delta(i)
It means that, if two fingers are moving +50 and +10 respectively, it will move +50; if they're moving at +50 and -10 respectively, it will move +40.
**When a pointer leaves**: 
  all pointers stop working.

**The fixed logic:**
A vector stores the EventMove of every pointer, **if the EventMove of all pointers reaches**, flush the vector and update the scroll delta. It means that, if three fingers (p1, p2, p3) are downing and moving: p1 down, p2 down, p3 down, p1 move +10, p2 move +20, and p3 move +30,  only three EventMoves reach(p1 EventMove, p2 EventMove, p3 EventMove), the DragGesture will move +30. 

**Additionally, if the EventMoves of one pointer reach continually,** then flush the vector directly. It means that, if three fingers are downing and moving: p1 down, p2 down, p3 down, p1 move +0, p2 move +0, and  p3 move +10, since the move deltas p1 and p2 both are 0, the EventMoves of p1 and p2 may not reach (according to the log of iOS), only the EventMove of p3 reached, the DragGesture will move +10 until the next EventMove of p3 reaches.

The approach will lost the last EventMove, for example, p1 down, p2 down, p1 move +0, p2 move+10, p1 move +0, p2 move +10,  p1 move +0, and p2 move +10, the Events: p1 EventDown, p2 EventDown, p2 EventMove, p2 EventMove, and p2 EventMove, the DragGesture will Move +10 and Move+10 finally. Actually, the EventMove of one pointer reaches many times, and the loss of the final EventMove is acceptable.

I modified the unit tests too, please have a review. Thank you!

The result videos have been attached below:

Improve fidelity of multi-pointer drag gestures #38926

> I wonder how panning is affected by this change. I'm mostly worried about the delta no longer being the change of the position, because this change wants to be compatible with both the iOS scrolling behavior and the iOS panning behavior. Maybe eventually we should have a "strategy" class, so that iOS monodrag, iOS panning, and Android dragging should have separate strategies.

I am confused about the iOS scrolling, the iOS panning, and the iOS monodrag what you mentioned, I am newer to iOS, after my investigation, it seems only the pan gesture is about scrolling in the picture below (Whether single finger or multiple).[UIPanGestureRecognizer](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/uikit/uipangesturerecognizer?language=objc). Can you give me more explanation? Thank you for your patience!
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/35736246/176476827-9e1e2d12-00fb-415a-a098-f7d0915d7b5a.png)

> Maybe eventually we should have a "strategy" class, so that iOS monodrag, iOS panning, and Android dragging should have separate strategies.

The logic in this fixed refers the iOS because the experience of iOS is better than Android. What I understand is you suggest different strategies (to simulate the Android and the iOS behavior) just like the over scroll physics behavior (the BouncingScrollPhysics and the ClampingScrollPhysics())? 

Thank you for your review!

I tried it out in my app, works well :thumbsup:

FYI Pan/zoom = trackpad scrolling event. Zoom/scale is not relevant here, only pan, which could also be called scroll. When I implemented it, I summed the deltas as previous behavior, but your solution is better in my opinion. The case of using both touchscreen and trackpad is not well handled by most platforms, trying it on iOS/UIKit causes a lot of flickering and is not correct, so there isn't any break with an existing standard to change it.

@moffatman 
> I tried it out in my app, works well 👍
> 
> FYI Pan/zoom = trackpad scrolling event. Zoom/scale is not relevant here, only pan, which could also be called scroll. When I implemented it, I summed the deltas as previous behavior, but your solution is better in my opinion.

>  The case of using both touchscreen and trackpad is not well handled by most platforms, trying it on iOS/UIKit causes a lot of flickering and is not correct, so there isn't any break with an existing standard to change it.

But I do not understand what's the problem with using a touchscreen, a lot of flickering on Andoird, iOS, or Windows surface? Can you give me some details about the devices to reproduce the problem? Thank you very much!
